title: Sophisticated discourse markers and register
lesson: Advanced cohesion depends on choosing the marker whose precise logical relation and register fit the context. Concessive 'nonetheless' and the very formal 'notwithstanding' (which can also be a preposition) override a preceding point; 'albeit' introduces a clipped concession; 'granted (that)' concedes before objecting. 'Conversely' flags an inverse relationship; 'insofar as' limits a claim to the extent something holds. Summative 'by and large' and 'to all intents and purposes' generalise; 'that said' pivots to qualification; 'all the more so' intensifies a reason just given.

items.text: The expedition was under-funded and poorly equipped; {1}, it reached the summit ahead of schedule.
items.explanation: 'Nonetheless' (and 'nevertheless') concedes the difficulties yet asserts a surprising result. 'Insofar as' restricts a claim and 'conversely' flags an opposite, neither of which fits a straightforward concession.

items.text: {1} the committee's reservations, the funding was approved in full.
items.explanation: 'Notwithstanding' here is a formal preposition equivalent to 'despite' or 'in spite of', taking a noun phrase directly. The alternatives are adverbial connectors and cannot govern 'the committee's reservations' this way.

items.text: Northern dialects lengthen the vowel in such words; {1}, southern speakers tend to clip it.
items.explanation: 'Conversely' signals that the second clause is the inverse of the first (lengthen vs clip). 'Albeit' introduces a concession and 'granted' a concessive admission, neither of which marks an opposite pattern.

items.text: The theory holds {1} we restrict it to ideal gases; beyond that, its predictions break down.

blanks.hint: 'to the extent that'; limits the claim's scope

items.explanation: 'Insofar as' delimits the extent to which a statement is true ('to the degree that'). The sentence restricts the theory's validity to a condition, which is exactly this marker's function.

items.text: She accepted the post, {1} with considerable reluctance, once the terms were improved.
items.explanation: 'Albeit' introduces a concessive phrase compactly (= 'though it was'), typically attaching to an adjective or adverbial, not a full clause with a new subject. 'By and large' generalises and does not fit a concession.

items.text: {1} that the data are incomplete, the trend they reveal is still unmistakable.

blanks.hint: concede a point first, then push back

items.explanation: 'Granted (that)' concedes an opposing point up front before the speaker presses on with their own ('...the trend is still unmistakable'). It sets up a concession-then-rebuttal that 'conversely' and 'insofar as' cannot.

items.text: The two firms have separate names, but {1} they now operate as a single company.

blanks.hint: 'in every practical respect', though not officially

items.explanation: 'To all intents and purposes' means 'in every practical sense, if not the formal one' — a perfect fit for entities that are technically distinct yet effectively one. The other markers do not carry this 'in practice' meaning.

items.text: There were a few dull stretches, but {1} the novel is gripping and beautifully paced.
items.explanation: 'By and large' generalises across the whole while allowing minor exceptions ('on the whole'). It fits the move from a small caveat to an overall verdict; the others do not generalise.

items.text: The new policy will cut costs significantly. {1}, it risks alienating long-standing customers.
items.explanation: 'That said' acknowledges what has just been stated and then pivots to a qualification or counterpoint ('having said that'). 'All the more so' would intensify the prior point rather than qualify it.

items.text: Mentoring matters for every junior employee, and {1} for those without a university background.

blanks.hint: intensifies the point just made for a special case

items.explanation: 'All the more so' strengthens a claim for a particular case — the reasoning applies, and even more forcefully here. 'That said' would reverse direction and 'conversely' would introduce an opposite, both wrong here.

items.text: Your objection is reasonable; {1}, the deadline is fixed and cannot be moved.
items.explanation: 'Be that as it may' concedes the validity of the previous statement while declaring it will not change the outcome ('even so'). It is more emphatic and formal than a plain 'but'; the alternatives do not perform this concede-and-override move.

items.text: The lecture, {1} brief, managed to cover every essential point in the syllabus.

blanks.hint: concessive 'although it was', fronting an adjective

items.explanation: 'Albeit' fits neatly before a single adjective ('albeit brief' = 'although it was brief'). 'Nonetheless' is a sentence adverb that cannot bridge directly into the adjective this way, and the summative phrase does not concede.
